segments.s258-g209: بیامد is past tense, 3rd‑person singular (‘he came’) and serves as the main verb. چو introduces a temporal clause here = 'when'. پیش has the sense 'in the presence of / before' and marks the position he reached. کنابد is an archaic/poetic 3rd‑person form meaning 'suffers / is in distress' and functions as the head of a descriptive clause ('the one who was suffering'). رسید is a past‑tense verb 'arrived/reached' that completes the temporal clause started by چو ('when he arrived before the suffering one').
segments.s258-g210: بر is a preposition here meaning 'on/upon' or 'at'. آن is the demonstrative 'that'. دامن کوه is a genitive phrase 'دامنِ کوه' = 'the mountain's skirt/foothills' (دامن used in the geographical sense). لشکر کشید is a verbal phrase: لشکر 'army' + کشید (past of کشیدن) meaning 'he drew up / deployed / marched (an army)'; in classical Persian لشکر‌کشیدن is an idiom for deploying or moving an army. (Note: the supplied sense list for کشید does not include the common meaning 'to draw/drag/deploy' so the correct sense index is not available.)
segments.s258-g121: گرین here is an archaic contraction meaning 'گر این' ('if these/if this'); it introduces the conditional clause. گفته‌های مرا is a possessive noun phrase 'my sayings/words' (گفته‌های + possessive clitic), and نشنوی is 2nd person singular present (used for the protasis of a conditional) meaning 'you do not hear/heed'. Word order is conditional marker + object phrase + verb.
segments.s258-g122: به فرجام is a prepositional phrase 'at/to the outcome'; کارت is 'your action/deed' (کار + second-person clitic), and پشیمان شوی is the apodosis: پشیمان (regretful) + شوی (2nd sg. of شدن 'become') meaning 'you will become regretful' or 'you will regret'. The verb is final as in classical Persian.
segments.s258-g123: پشیمانی is the subject 'regret'; آنگه is an adverb 'then/at that time'; نداردت is the verb ندارد 'has not' with the enclitic -ت marking the person 'you' (so literally 'it has for you not'), and سود is the predicate noun 'benefit'. The clause means 'regret then will have no benefit for you.'
segments.s258-g124: که here introduces the reason/clause ('for/because'). تیغ زمانه is a genitive 'the sword of time/fortune' (تیغ + زمانه). سرت را places the definite object 'your head' before the verb (سرت + object marker را). درود is the verb in final position meaning to strike/cut (an older/poetic verbal form); the line says the sword of fate will strike your head.
segments.s258-g125: بگفت is simple past 'he said'. The order here is somewhat inverted and poetic: 'این سخن' (these words) is the direct object, 'پهلوان' is the subject 'the hero/champion', and 'با پسر' indicates the addressee ('to his son' or 'with his son' in older usage). So: 'The hero said these words to his son.'
segments.s258-g126: که functions as a complementizer 'that' introducing the reported clause. برخوان is a locative/occasional noun 'at the table/feast', به پیران 'among/to the elders', همه 'all/everyone', and دربه‌در is an adjectival predicate 'homeless/destitute'. The clause states the situation at the feast among the elders: everyone was destitute.
segments.s258-g127: ز پیش پدر is a prepositional phrase 'from before/from the presence of his father' (ز + پیش). گیو شد is a compact past construction 'Giv departed/left' (name + شد); تا + به بلخ indicates the destination 'to Balkh' (تا used here as 'as far as/to').
segments.s258-g128: گرفته is a past-participial adjective 'seized/taken (with emotion)', here describing Giv's state. به یاد 'in the memory of' (lit. 'by memory'), آن سخنهای 'those words/speeches', تلخ 'bitter' (figurative). Together: 'seized by the remembrance of those bitter words.'
segments.s258-g129: فرود آمد is a past simple verbal phrase literally 'came down' (here 'dismounted' or 'alighted') and is followed by و + a second past verb (فرستاد), so two actions are linked: 'he dismounted and sent'. کس is an indefinite direct object 'someone'; فرستاد is simple past 'sent'. زود is an adverb 'quickly/at once' placed after the verb to modify فرستاد.
segments.s258-g130: بر آن سان که is a fixed comparative phrase meaning 'in that manner / just as'. گودرز is the agent name; فرموده بود is a compound past: فرموده is a perfective/participial form meaning 'ordered/said' and بود is the past auxiliary, giving a pluperfect/reportive nuance 'had commanded/said'. The whole clause is a subordinate simile specifying how he sent the messenger.
segments.s258-g131: همان شب is a time adverbial 'that very night'. سپاه is the direct object 'the army'. اندر آورد + گرد here form a classical verbal cluster meaning 'gathered/assembled' (literal components 'brought in' + گرد), so word order puts the time phrase first and the compound verb at the end: 'that night (he) brought the army together.'
segments.s258-g132: برفت is a past tense verb 'set off / went forth' (an older/formal past form); از در بلخ is a prepositional phrase 'from the gate of Balkh' where در here means 'gate'. تا introduces the limit/destination 'as far as / to' and ویسه گرد is a toponym (Viseh‑gird) treated as the endpoint. The sequence gives departure and destination.
segments.s258-g133: که introduces an explanatory subordinate clause 'for / that'. پیران is plural 'the elders'. The sequenceِ "بدان شهر" in the manuscript is compressed here; taken together it means 'in that city' (بدان = 'in that'), and بد با سپاه means 'ill-disposed/hostile toward the army' where بد qualifies the attitude. The clause says the elders of that city were unfriendly to the army.
segments.s258-g134: که again continues the explanatory sequence. دیهیم ایران is a genitive/compound meaning 'the rural folk / villagers of Iran'. همی is the older continuous particle meaning 'still/continuously'. جست is a past form here meaning 'sought / were searching' (classical usage). و گاه ('and at times') adds a temporal nuance; the clause portrays the country folk as actively seeking or restless.
segments.s258-g135: فرستاده is the subject 'the messenger'. چون here functions as a temporal conjunction 'when/as'. سوی پیران is a prepositional phrase 'toward/to the elders'. رسید is past 'arrived/reached', so the whole clause reads 'when the messenger reached (came to) the elders'.
segments.s258-g136: سپهدار پیران is a nominal phrase 'the commander (سپهدار) of the elders' (genitive relation by juxtaposition). سپه را uses را as the definite object marker marking سپه ('the army') as object, and بدید is simple past 'saw'. Together: 'the elders' commander saw the army.'
segments.s258-g185: بگویش is the imperative verb بگو + enclitic -یش meaning 'tell him' (second-person imperative addressing the speaker to order someone to tell a third person). که introduces the subordinate clause. The clause از من تو چیزی مجوی literally orders 'from me you (تو) nothing seek-not': از من = 'from me', تو is emphatic subject 'you', چیزی = 'anything/anything at all' as the object, and مجوی is the negative jussive/imperfect form 'do not seek' (classical/poetic negative of جستن).
segments.s258-g186: که introduces a relative / restrictive clause 'that/which'. فرزانگان is the plural 'the sages, the wise men' who are the external standard. آن refers back to 'چیزی' ('that/it'). نبینند is the third-person plural present/subjunctive negative of دیدن meaning 'they do not / would not see' (here a classical jussive sense: 'that the sages should not see'). روی here means 'face' ('see the face of it' = 'perceive it')—an idiomatic way to say 'they would not behold it'.
segments.s258-g187: یکی functions as an enumerative 'one' or 'first (point)'. آنکه ('that which' / 'the fact that') introduces the content of this point. از نامدار گوان is a prepositional phrase 'from the renowned ones/from the famed' (از = 'from', نامدار = 'renowned', گوان here indicates 'ones of that sort' or 'such people' in a classical poetic pluralizing/collective sense).
segments.s258-g188: گروگان is the object 'a hostage/pledge'. همی is the older poetic present-marker/emphatic particle (imperfective continuous). خواهی is second-person singular present/future 'you want/you will ask' (here 'you would demand/seek'). این کی توان is a short exclamatory question: این = 'this', کی here functions as 'who/how' in classical usage ('who/which can' or 'how can'), and توان is the auxiliary 'can/be able'. Together they mean 'how can this be?' or 'who can do this?'.
segments.s258-g189: و دیگر = 'and another' / 'and moreover'. که گفتی here is a relative or explanatory 'that you said' (گفتی is the 2nd-person past/present perfect 'you said' in classical usage; contextually it introduces what was mentioned). سلیح و سپاه lists what was named: سلیح = 'weapons/armaments', سپاه = 'army/host'.
segments.s258-g190: گرانمایه is an attributive adjective 'valuable/precious' placed before the nouns it modifies. اسبان و تخت و کلاه are the coordinated nouns 'horses, throne, and hats/helmets' (و = 'and'), forming a piled list of the costly items mentioned.
segments.s258-g191: برادر here is a noun of address or description 'a brother' (can be vocative). که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. روشن جهان is a compound attributive phrase 'the light (brightness) of the world' (روشن = 'bright/illuminating', جهان = 'the world'). منست is an archaic/enclitic copular/possessive formula (classical contraction meaning 'is mine' / 'belongs to me') rather than the modern separate 'من است'; thus the clause states that 'a brother who is the world's light belongs to me'.
segments.s258-g192: گزیده پسر is a noun phrase 'the chosen son' (گزیده = 'selected/chosen', پسر = 'son/youth'). پهلوان منست follows as a predication 'is my champion' (پهلوان = 'champion/hero', and again منست functions as the archaic copula/possessive 'is mine'). The two halves are parallel appositions describing the person's relation to the speaker.
segments.s258-g41: نباید که introduces an impersonal negative modal clause ('it should not be that / you should not'). "بر دستِ ما" is a prepositional phrase meaning 'at/by our hand' (دست here = power/authority), placed before the verb; تباه is a predicative adjective that the verb in the next segment will complete (classical Persian often places the adjective before the verb 'شوی').
segments.s258-g42: شوی is the 2nd-person singular subjunctive/present of شدن used here to mean 'you be / become' (completing تباه = 'be ruined'). "بر گذشته فراوان گناه" is a causal/ circumstantial phrase 'on account of / because of many past sins' — گذشته = 'past' modifying گناه, and فراوان qualifies گناه.
segments.s258-g43: دگر is an adverb meaning 'again / moreover' introducing a new point. کز is the classical contraction (که + از) used to introduce a subordinate clause here ('that/which / when'), and پی جنگِ افراسیاب literally means 'in the wake of / following the war of Afrasiab' (پی = 'after/following', جنگِ افراسیاب = genitive 'Afrasiab's war').
segments.s258-g44: زمانه functions as an agent ('Fortune' / 'the times') rather than merely 'era.' همی is the classical continuous/habitual marker (present progressive flavor). گیرد (گرفت#گیر) is a present-tense transitive verb 'takes/seizes', and شتاب is the thing taken ('haste' / 'rapid pressure'); بر تو = 'upon you'. Word order is somewhat inverted (agent — aspect marker — object — verb).
segments.s258-g45: A coordinated noun phrase in subject position: بزرگانِ ایران = 'the great ones / elders of Iran' and فرزندِ من = 'my son' (postnominal من marks possession). The two nouns are joined by و and will serve as the agents of the following verb.
segments.s258-g46: بخوانند is a jussive/subjunctive 3rd-person-plural form meaning 'let them recite/say' or 'they should recite.' بر تو marks the indirect object 'to/for you.' همه پندِ من = 'all my advice/counsels' (همه = all; پند = advice; من = possessive 'my' after the noun).
segments.s258-g47: سخن is the direct object 'words / speech.' هرچ دانی is a relative/indefinite phrase 'whatever you know' (هرچ = whatever; دانی = 'you know'). بدیشان = 'to them' (بـ + ایشان), and بگوی is the jussive/imperative 'say / tell' directed at the addressee (classical second-person singular imperative).
segments.s258-g48: وز here is a poetic fusion (و + از) meaning 'and from.' ایشان = 'them.' همیدون is a proper name (Hamidun); سخن = 'the matter / speech' (object), and بازجوی is an imperative form of بازجستن 'inquire / ask' (lit. 'ask/investigate'). The whole clause means 'and from them, ask Hamidun about the matter' (i.e., go to Hamidun for further inquiry).
segments.s258-g153: بزرگان ایران is a noun phrase (plural noun + postposed genitive) meaning 'the great/elder men of Iran.' زمان یافتند is a verb phrase where زمان (here 'the moment, opportunity') is the direct object and یافتند is the past plural of یافتن ('they found'); Persian often places the object before the verb.
segments.s258-g154: بر ایشان is a prepositional phrase 'toward/at them' (بر here 'toward/against/at'); به گفتار is an instrumental/adverbial phrase 'with speech / to speak' (به + گفتار gives 'by/with speech' or 'to say'), and بشتافتند is past plural 'they hastened/ran'—so together 'they hurried toward them to speak.' Word order is prepositional phrase(s) before the verb, as is common.
segments.s258-g155: برافگند is the past plural form of a verbal root meaning 'they threw off / they scattered / they cast (away),' here used of the elders (پیران). هم means 'also/too' and اندر شتاب is an adverbial phrase 'in haste' (اندر = 'in/within', شتاب = 'haste'). The clause therefore indicates the elders likewise acted quickly.
segments.s258-g156: نوندی is an isolated, somewhat ambiguous noun here (in classical Persian poetry it can name the musical mode 'Navand' or a rare proper name); it stands before به نزدیک افراسیاب, where به نزدیک means 'toward/near' and افراسیاب is the place/name. Syntactically the noun phrase is placed before the locative phrase, so the line reads that the Navand (or person called Navand) was at/near Afrasiab—or that they chanted/performed the Navand near Afrasiab (a poetic, elliptical wording).
segments.s258-g157: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('that' / 'when' / 'for'); گودرز is the proper name 'Goudarz' and کشوادگان is an epithet/title attached to him (an archaic/uncertain term here). با سپاه is the prepositional phrase 'with the army' (با + سپاه) indicating accompaniment. The whole segment sets up the agent and his force.
segments.s258-g158: نهاد is the past of نهادن ('he put/placed' but in epic usage can mean 'he set/removed/caused'), از بر تخت is a prepositional/locative phrase 'from upon the throne' (از = 'from', بر = 'on/upon', تخت = 'throne'), and گردان کلاه is a noun phrase where گردان here is best read as adjectival 'revolving/turning' (or descriptive) modifying کلاه 'hat/crown/helmet'. Object position (کلاه) after the verb is typical poetic word order; context yields the meaning 'he (took/removed) the crown/helmet from the throne.'
segments.s258-g159: فرستاده آمد is a straightforward clause 'a messenger came' (فرستاده = 'messenger' or 'one sent', آمد = past 'came'). به نزدیک من is a locative phrase 'near me / to my proximity' (به = 'toward', نزدیک = 'near', من = 'me').
segments.s258-g160: گزین پور او is a nominal phrase 'the chosen son of him' (گزین 'chosen/selected' used adjectivally, پور 'son' follows, and او 'of him' functions as the possessive reference). مهتر انجمن is another nominative phrase 'the chief/elder of the assembly' (مهتر = 'chief/eldest', انجمن = 'assembly/council'); the copula 'is/was' is omitted as normal in Persian poetry, so the two phrases identify the messenger.
segments.s258-g49: This is a prototypical conditional clause: 'اگر ... باشد' uses the present/subjunctive form 'باشد' to mean 'if it be/if it is'. Word order is slightly inverted: the predicative adjective 'راست' ('true/sincere') is linked to the possessor 'دلت' (your heart), and the instrumental phrase 'با زبان' ('with/by the tongue' = 'in your speech') specifies that sincerity is shown outwardly. The 2nd-person clitic 'ت' on 'دل' marks possession: 'your heart'.
segments.s258-g50: Two coordinated past/result verbs describe the consequence: 'گذشتی' (you have passed/you passed) + 'و' + 'رستی' (you are saved/delivered). 'ز تیمار' is a prepositional phrase 'from/won over worry/care', and 'به جان' literally 'to/for life' means 'in life' or 'alive'. Grammatically, both verbs agree with the 2nd-person subject implied by the previous clause.
segments.s258-g51: This clause has a compound subject 'بر و بوم و خویشانت' ('land and dwelling and your relatives') followed by the predicative adjective 'آباد' and the past verb 'گشت' ('became'). 'آباد گشت' = 'became prosperous/inhabited'. The conjunctions 'و' simply join the coordinate nouns.
segments.s258-g52: Structure: a causative/agent phrase 'ز تیغ منت' ('from the sword [and its] ...') is followed by the result 'گردن آزاد گشت' ('the neck became free'). Poetically 'تیغ منّت' refers to the imposition/pressure associated with the sword (i.e. violent domination), and 'گردن آزاد گشت' means 'the neck was freed' (freedom from bondage/submission).
segments.s258-g53: 'ور' is a literary conditional 'if'. The verb phrase 'پدیدار آید' (the noun 'پدیدار' + verb 'آید') means 'appears/comes into view'; word order places the source 'از تو' ('from you') between the conditional particle and the predicate. Grammatically this reads 'If a fault appears from you...'.
segments.s258-g54: The negative/result clause uses the perfective 'نماند' ('will not remain/there will be none left') with the dative 'به تو' ('for you'). A triad 'مهر و تخت و کلاه' ('love/affection, throne, and hat/crown') is listed as what will be lost. Word order places the verb before its objects for emphasis, typical of poetic style.
segments.s258-g55: The verb 'نجویم' is first-person singular with prefixed negative 'ن' + subjunctive/present ('جویم'), meaning 'I do not seek / I will not seek'. 'بر این کینه' is an adverbial phrase 'on account of this grudge', and 'آرام و خواب' are the abstract objects 'rest and sleep'. Together: 'I will not seek rest or sleep because of this grudge.'
segments.s258-g56: This is a list of the parties/dependent things affected by that denial: 'من و گرز و میدان افراسیاب' — 'myself, (my) mace, and Afrasiab's field'. 'میدان افراسیاب' is a genitive phrase 'the field/arena of Afrasiab'. In context the line means the speaker will allow no rest for himself, his weapon, or the battle-ground.
segments.s258-g65: به سان is a prepositional phrase meaning “like / in the manner of” with به functioning adverbially; it precedes the noun phrase سگانش (سگان + enclitic -ش “his”), so the whole phrase reads “like his dogs.” از آن انجمن is a simple prepositional phrase “from that assembly/gathering,” with آن as the demonstrative modifying انجمن.
segments.s258-g66: ببندی and فرستی are two verbs in the 2nd person singular form (classical/subjunctive/imperative flavor): ببندی = “bind/close/tie up” and فرستی = “send/dispatch.” They form a sequence of actions; به نزدیک من is a directional/prepositional phrase “toward/near me” (به + adjective نزدیک + pronoun من as object).
segments.s258-g67: بدان is the imperative of دانستن with the prefix ب- (ب + دان) meaning “know (this).” تا here is a purposive marker “so that / in order that,” introducing the clause فرستم (1st person present/subjunctive or simple future) “I will send / I shall dispatch.” به نزدیک شاه is the directional phrase “near the king.”
segments.s258-g68: This is a paired interrogative/alternating clause using چه ... چه …: the first چه introduces one possibility and the second چه introduces the other. شان here is a possessive/enclitic sense (“their”) attached to the following noun, so چه شان سر ستاند reads “what (he) will take of their heads” (ستاند is an older/poetic verb form ‘takes/snatches’). بخشد is present/subjunctive “he gives/bestows,” and کلاه is literal “hat,” used here as a mark of granting/office. The repetition چه … چه … lists the two possible outcomes the speaker will observe.
segments.s258-g69: تو is the 2nd-person singular pronoun “you.” نشنیدی is the past (perfect) negative form meaning “you did not hear” or “you have not heard.” آن داستان بزرگ is the direct object: آن = “that,” داستان = “tale/story,” بزرگ = “great.”
segments.s258-g70: که introduces a relative clause “which / that.” شیر is “lion.” ژیان here is an epithet attached to شیر (classical/poetic usage) and can be read as part of the lion’s epithet. آورد is past tense “brought” (here “related / brought [the tale]”), and پیش گرگ is a prepositional phrase meaning “before/in the presence of the wolf” (پیش = before/in front of, گرگ = wolf).
segments.s258-g71: که is the subordinating conjunction “for / that.” هر کو is an older compound meaning “whoever” (هر = every/any; کو is an archaic relative here). به خون کیان = “with / in the blood of the kings” (به + خون + کیان), and دست آخت is a poetic/periphrastic past: دست = “hand,” آخت is an archaic verb meaning “to lay on / to take / to stain” — together meaning “laid hands on / stained (one’s) hands.”
segments.s258-g72: زمانه here means “time” or “the age/world” (the cosmic/fortune sense). به جز خاک = “except earth/dust” (به جز = except), جایش is جای + enclitic ش = “his/its place,” and نساخت is the past (negative form in surface shape) of ساخت “to make / to grant” used here in the sense “(time) made for him nothing but dust” — classical word order places the exception (به جز خاک) before the verb.
segments.s258-g193: همی + گویی is a habitual present: همی (an adverb) indicates continuing action and گویی here functions as 'you say/you keep saying' (classical present). از خویشتن دور کن is an imperative clause: از خویشتن 'away/from yourself' and دور کن 'make (it) distant' = 'keep yourself away'. Word order is typical older Persian: adverb → verb → prepositional phrase → imperative.
segments.s258-g194: ز بخرد چنین خام باشد سخن is an evaluative comment: ز (= از, 'from/for') بخرد 'from a wise man' (بخرد = 'the wise one'), چنین خام 'such raw/immature' سخن 'speech/words'. باشد is the classical auxiliary/optative form ('is / would be') linking the predicate to the subject; overall: 'For a wise man such words are raw/naive.'
segments.s258-g195: مرا is the experiencer/indirect object ('to me' / 'for me' — here written as م + را), followed by the nominal sentence مرگ بهتر از آن زندگی: 'death (مرگ) [is] better (بهتر) than (از) that life (آن زندگی)'. Persian often omits the copula, so 'is' is understood.
segments.s258-g196: که introduces a relative/clausal phrase modifying 'that life' = 'that life in which...'. سالار باشم is a subjunctive/present-tense clause 'that I am/should be a lord' (باشم is the 1st‑person subjunctive/capability form). کنم بندگی follows with کنم 'I do/make' + بندگی 'servitude' = 'I perform/be engaged in servitude'. The clause contrasts external rank (سالار) with the internal state of servitude.
segments.s258-g197: یکی داستان زد is an idiom: یکی 'a certain one' + داستان زد (داستان زدن) = 'told a tale'. بر این بر پلنگ uses بر ... بر to mark the object/topic 'about this panther' (poetic repetition of بر). Word order here is topical and compact: 'A certain tale was told concerning this panther.'
segments.s258-g198: چو 'when' introduces the temporal clause. با شیر جنگ = 'a fight with the lion' (با + شیر + جنگ). آورش is the verbal/agentive element from آور (here functioning as a formative: 'bringing/causing fight' or 'to make war upon him'), and خاست (past) = 'arose/began'. The final جنگ reiterates 'battle/war'—so: 'when it rose up to make war with the lion, battle began.'
segments.s258-g199: This segment contains reported speech introduced elliptically. به نام 'for the sake of a name/reputation'; ار (if) + بریزی (present/subjunctive or second-person singular: 'you spill/pour') gives the conditional. مرا is the indirect object 'to me' (the person addressed: 'he said to me'), and گفت is the past 'said' introducing the quoted counsel; خون 'blood' begins the content of what was said. Word order is compressed: the reported conditional appears folded into the main report.
segments.s258-g200: The comparative formula خون به از زندگانی ... uses 'X به از Y' = 'X is better than Y'. Thus 'blood is better than life (زندگانی) ... به ننگ اندرون' — the final phrase 'به ننگ اندرون' = 'with shame inside' or 'inwardly stained with disgrace'. The structure places the preferred item (blood) before the comparative marker 'به از' and the thing compared.
segments.s258-g9: سپهدار پس گیو را پیش خواند is a short clause: سپهدار (the commander) is the subject placed first for emphasis; پس here means 'then/thereupon'. گیو را uses را as the definite/object marker marking Giv as the direct object. پیش خواند is a compound verbal idea meaning 'called/summoned forward' (پیش = forward/in the presence of; خواند here means 'to call/summon').
segments.s258-g10: همه گفته شاه با او براند functions as the object/command accompanying the summons: همه گفته شاه = 'all that the king has said' (همه = the whole/entire, گفته = 'what was said'); با او = 'with him' or 'to him' (با = with); براند is an archaic/poetic present/subjunctive-like form of راندن used here in the sense 'bring/escort/convey' (i.e. 'see that he takes/conveys it').
segments.s258-g11: بدو گفت = به + او + گفت: بدو is the dative 'to him' (به او contracted) and گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (here introducing direct speech). کای is a vocative 'O' (address), and پور سالار is an honorific apposition: پور = 'son (of)' used as part of an epithet and سالار = 'commander/chief'. The final سر begins the succeeding descriptive phrase ('head').
segments.s258-g12: برافراخته سر ز بسیار سر is a compact epithet: برافراخته (raised, proud) modifies سر (head). ز is the poetic/prepositional 'from/because of' used here to mean 'above' or 'over'; بسیار سر = 'many heads' (بسیار = many). Together the phrase says the person's head is raised high above many others.
segments.s258-g13: گزین کردم is a first‑person past compound: گزین 'to choose/select' + کردم 'I did' = 'I chose'. اندر is the archaic/pre‑classical preposition 'in/within' or 'for'. خورت here is problematic/archaic (not covered by the provided senses); in context it functions as a possessive/dative idea 'for your (use/command)'. لشکری is the noun 'troops/army' being selected.
segments.s258-g14: که هستند سالار هر کشوری is a relative clause modifying لشکری: که = 'who/that', هستند = 'are' (third‑person plural), سالار = 'leaders/commanders', هر = 'every/each', کشوری = 'of the country/realm'. Literally: 'who are the commanders of every realm.'
segments.s258-g15: بدان is the imperative/infinitive 'know this' formed from the prefix ب (here the verbal imperative prefix be-) + دان (to know). تا = 'so that/in order that'. به نزدیک پیران شوی: به = 'to/toward', نزدیک = 'near', پیران = 'the elders', شوی is subjunctive/optative of شدن 'become' meaning 'may you come/approach' (shifts the clause into purpose/result: 'so that you may approach the elders').
segments.s258-g16: بگویی و گفتار او بشنوی contains two coordinated imperatives/purposes: بگویی (here 'you should say' or 'speak') — the verb form is the subjunctive/infinitival use of گفتن used for reported/intentional speech — و = 'and', گفتار او = 'his speech/words' (گفتار = speech; او = 'his'), بشنوی = subjunctive of شنیدن 'you may hear'. Together: 'speak and hear his words.'
segments.s258-g1: This is a noun phrase: بی‌آزار is an adjective before the noun لشکر, and به فرمان شاه is a prepositional modifier ('at the king's command') placed after the noun. Word order (adj + noun + prepositional phrase) is normal in classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s258-g2: همی + past verb رفت is a classical/poetic continuous or iterative past (marks ongoing or habitual movement: 'kept going' / 'marched'). منزل به منزل is an idiomatic adverbial phrase 'from camp/stage to camp/stage'. The subject سپاه appears at the end (poetic inversion) rather than before the verb.
segments.s258-g3: چو is a subordinating conjunction 'when'; it introduces the time clause. گودرز is the subject. نزدیک + (place) here reads as 'near' (approaching); زیبد in this context is a place-name (so it functions as the oblique locative), and رسید is the past tense verb 'arrived/reached'.
segments.s258-g4: سران را: سران 'leaders' is the definite/accusative object marked by را. ز لشکر means 'from (among) the army' (ز = 'from'). همی before the verb برگزید gives the classical continuous/iterative shade to the past verb; برگزید is the past tense 'chose/selected'.
segments.s258-g5: هزاران is a quantifier 'thousands (of)'; it precedes the plural noun دلیران 'the brave ones'. خنجر گزار is a compound meaning 'dagger-bearers' (خنجر 'dagger' + گزار as an agentive element). The whole phrase is a nominal group; Persian often omits a copula in such listings.
segments.s258-g6: ز گردان لشکر = 'from the battalions/companies of the army' (ز = 'from', گردان = 'troop/unit', لشکر = 'army'). دلاور سوار is a noun phrase 'valiant horsemen' where دلاور qualifies سوار; the whole segment further specifies the kind of men just named.
segments.s258-g7: از ایرانیان نامور is a prepositional/partitive phrase 'from the renowned Iranians'; ده‌هزار is the numeric quantifier 'ten thousand' that specifies how many. The order (from X, number) is typical in enumerative passages.
segments.s258-g8: سخن گوی is a compound meaning 'speakers' or 'eloquent (men of) speech' (سخن 'speech' + گوی 'sayer/speaker'). و اندر introduces the next quality 'and among/in' or 'and (they were) fit for'; خور here is an older/poetic adjective meaning 'fit/apt' (not one of the simple modern senses listed), and کارزار means 'battle/fight'. Together they describe these men as eloquent and ready for battle.
segments.s258-g137: بگفتند (they said) is a past reporting verb introducing a reported clause. کآمد is an older/poetic form of آمد (here functioning like a subordinating/relative particle + past: “that came / had come”), and سوی بلخ گیو is the directional phrase (سوی = toward; بلخ = Balkh) with the proper name گیو as the subject of the reported clause.
segments.s258-g138: This is a nominal apposition/titling phrase: ابا functions here as a name or name‑element (not the lexical verb “refuse”), ویژگان is an epithet meaning “the distinguished,” and سپهدار is a title meaning “commander,” followed by نیو (a name). The sequence lists name/epithet/title in a compact, poetic order.
segments.s258-g139: چو introduces a temporal clause (چو = when). بشنید is the past tense ‘he heard’ and پیران here is the proper name Piran (the agent). برافراخت is a past/preterite verb compound meaning “hoisted/raised” (here used with کوس = war‑drum, i.e., he sounded the drum). The verb follows the subject in this classical narrative order.
segments.s258-g140: شد is the copular/predicative past ‘became’ and introduces the result clause. از marks cause/agent (“by/from the hooves”), سم اسبان is a genitive construction meaning “the hooves of the horses,” and زمین آبنوس is a nominative phrase placed after the verb (typical poetic inversion) meaning “the earth [became] like ebony” — آبنوس used metaphorically for very dark/black.
segments.s258-g141: ده و دو هزارش is a numeral phrase: ده و دو = twelve, هزارش = ‘his thousand(s)’ with the possessive/-sh suffix, so ‘his twelve thousand.’ ز (poetic variant of از) لشکر indicates source ‘from the host/army,’ and سوار (placed after لشکر) specifies they are mounted horsemen.
segments.s258-g142: فراز آمد is a compound meaning ‘rose up / came up (on high)’ with فراز as an adverbial/locative element and آمد the verb ‘came.’ اندر is the preposition ‘into/in,’ and خور کارزار is a poetic locution meaning the place/face of the battlefield (خور used poetically); together they mean ‘came up into the battlefield.’
segments.s258-g143: از ایشان = ‘of them / from those,’ دو بهره = ‘two shares/parts’ (i.e., two groups left behind), هم = ‘also/indeed,’ آنجا = ‘there,’ and بماند is the verb ‘remained/was left’ (classical/paratactic present‑form used narratively for past result). The whole clause says two groups stayed behind.
segments.s258-g144: برفت is an archaic past form meaning ‘he went away/left’ (not the noun ‘snow’), و = ‘and/then,’ جهاندیدگان is a plural noun meaning ‘the worldly‑wise / battle‑seasoned men’ (here: veterans), را marks them as the definite direct object, and بخواند is the verb ‘he called/summoned’ (a classical third‑person past/performative form used in narrative).
segments.s258-g17: بگویی is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive (jussive) form used here as an instruction ('you should tell' / 'tell'). به پیران places the indirect object after the verb and uses به + plural noun for 'to the elders'. که introduces the reported clause that follows; من با سپاه is the topic of that subordinate clause ('I, with the army').
segments.s258-g18: به زیبد رسیدم is a first-person past tense clause: رسیدم is 'I arrived/reached' (simple past). زیبد here functions adverbially ('fittingly' / 'properly' / 'as is fitting') with the preceding به marking manner, and به فرمان شاه uses به with the third sense ('by') to indicate agency or instrument: 'by the king's command.' The verb comes before the instrumental phrase in this classical word order.
segments.s258-g19: شناسی here is an exhortative/present-form of the root شناخت used as an imperative-like verb ('know' / 'be aware'); تو is the subject pronoun 'you' (emphatic). گفتار و کردار خویش is a coordinated object phrase 'your speech and your conduct' where خوی + ش (=خویش) marks possession ('your own').
segments.s258-g20: بی at the start is the prepositional prefix 'without' and governs the following coordinated nouns (آزاری و رنج و تیمار). This constructs a series of things to be absent ('without harm, without suffering, without care'). تیمار خویش is a noun + possessive (خوی + ش) meaning 'one's own care' or 'self-care' — here negated by بی. The list is linked by و as simple coordination.
segments.s258-g21: همه is a universal quantifier placed before the noun phrase شهر توران to yield 'all the towns of Turan' (modifier + noun + genitive). بدی را is the definite object marked by the object marker را, and میان follows to indicate location/position 'in the middle' or 'between' (i.e. they confined/placed the evil amid them).
segments.s258-g22: ببستند is the past plural 'they closed / they bound / they shut' (classical b- prefix + past stem). با introduces accompaniment or association ('with' / 'together with'), so با نامدار کیان reads 'with the renowned Kian(s)' — either indicating collaboration or accompaniment. Named nouns follow the preposition in normal order.
segments.s258-g23: فریدون فرخ is the personal name plus the epithet فرخ ('Farrokh' = 'fortunate' used appositively). که introduces a relative clause 'who...' and با داغ و درد is a prepositional phrase 'with/through burning grief and sorrow' specifying cause or circumstance.
segments.s258-g24: ز is the classical poetic preposition 'from' or 'because of' (here causal/'through/from the world' tone), گیتی 'world' follows it. بشد is the classical past form of شدن 'to become' (here 'became' / 'came to be'). دیده is the logical subject 'the eye(s)'; پر is an adjective 'full (of)'; آب زرد is a noun phrase 'yellow water' (i.e. tears described as 'yellow water'). Classical word order places the verb بشد before the predicate describing the eyes.
segments.s258-g169: The correlative negation 'نه ... نه ...' enumerates items that are excluded. 'باید' is a modal 'should/ought to' and governs the subordinate clause introduced by 'که'. In classical/poetic Persian the subjunctive verb often appears without the prefix بـ; here 'ماند' functions as the subjunctive 'remain/stay' required by 'باید که'. Word order is Name — modal — subordinator — verb ('گودرز باید که ماند').
segments.s258-g170: Continuation of the same negative enumeration using 'نه' before each grouped name. 'فرهاد و گرگین' is a coordinated pair (Farhad and Gorgin). The final 'نه رهام نیو' repeats the negator and lists another name phrase; here 'نیو' is presented as a name (not handled in the provided sense set).
segments.s258-g171: 'که' is the subordinating conjunction 'that' linking this clause to what precedes. 'بر ما سپه آمد' is literally 'an army came upon/against us' (preposition 'بر' = 'against/upon', subject 'سپه' before the verb, verb 'آمد' at the end as typical SOV order). 'از چار سوی' is a postverbal locative phrase 'from the four directions' with 'از' + numeral + 'سوی' ('سوی' = 'side/direction').
segments.s258-g172: 'همی گاه' is an adverbial durative/temporal expression meaning something like 'ever/constantly' or 'at times with continuity' (همی adds durative force). 'توران' is the subject (Turan, i.e. the Turanians). 'کنند' is 3rd person plural present/optative 'they do/make', and it governs the noun 'آرزوی' (object) which here means 'wish/longing' — the verb + noun order is a poetic inversion of the more regular 'آرزو کنند'.
segments.s258-g173: 'جفا پیشه گشتم' is an idiomatic nominal phrase meaning 'I became a maker/practitioner of cruelty' ('پیشه' = practice/profession, 'گشتم' = I became). 'از این پس' is a fixed adverbial phrase 'from this on/henceforth'. 'به جنگ' here marks the domain or the arena ('in/for war').
segments.s258-g174: The verb 'نجویم' (from جویدن/جست‌و‌جو) appears here in first person form and, with the infinitival phrase that follows, conveys 'I will not refrain/hesitate' (poetic/elliptical use). 'به خون ریختن' is an infinitival/masdar construction 'to spill blood' (object of the verb); 'بر درنگ' is an adverbial prepositional phrase literally 'on delay' but idiomatically 'without delay' — together the clause says the speaker will not delay or hesitate in shedding blood.
segments.s258-g175: 'به رای' means 'by the counsel/opinion of' (the preposition 'به' + noun 'رای'). 'هشیوار' modifies 'رای' as 'prudent/alert' — 'به رای هشیوار' = 'by the counsel of the prudent'. The coordinated phrase 'و مردان مرد' is a fixed laudatory expression 'and the men of men' (i.e., the bravest/most heroic men), where the second 'مرد' intensifies the first.
segments.s258-g176: 'برآرم' is 1st-person singular (poetic/future) 'I will raise/bring up/gather' (برآوردن/برآوردن → برآرد/برآرم). 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from/of' so 'ز کیخسرو' means 'from Kay Khosrow' (i.e., raised on his behalf or out of his resources). 'این بار' is 'this time/occasion'. The final word 'گرد' here refers to the gathered force/round (an archaic poetic use meaning a body/host); its exact dictionary senses do not list 'host' explicitly, so it is treated as an idiomatic/archaic noun in context.
segments.s258-g161: مرا (m+را) is the marked possessor/object ('my' / 'to me') placed before the possessed phrase; گوش و دل is a coordinated noun phrase 'ear and heart' (literal + figurative 'attention and heart'). سوی فرمان is a prepositional phrase 'toward the command'. تست is the poetic/copulative ending (classical 'تو هست' / 'thou art' or 'is yours') placed at the end to link the predicate to the subject. Word order here is topicalized (possessor + nouns + direction + copula).
segments.s258-g162: به پیمان is an oblique/locative/instrument phrase 'by/under the covenant'; روانم is 'my soul' with the possessive clitic -م; گروگان is a predicative noun 'hostage/pledge'. The final تست again functions as the copula linking predicate to the implied possessor ('is [yours]'), so the sequence reads 'by the covenant my soul [is] a hostage.'
segments.s258-g163: سخن is the subject 'the report/news'. چون here is a classical conjunction meaning 'when/as soon as' (conjunctive temporal use), introducing the temporal clause. به سالار ترکان is the prepositional phrase 'to the commander of the Turks' (سالار governing the genitive ترکان). رسید is the past tense verb 'reached/arrived' placed at the clause end, as is normal in Persian.
segments.s258-g164: سپاهی is the object 'a troop/army' that follows; ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' selecting a subset; جنگ‌آوران is a compound 'war‑bringers' = 'warriors' (آوران: bringers/doers attached to جنگ). برگزید is the past tense 'he chose/selected' and governs the preceding object, so the verb occurs at the end of this clause.
segments.s258-g165: فرستاد is the past-tense verb 'sent/dispatched'. نزدیک modifies location 'near/at the vicinity of' and is followed by پیران (here a proper name, Piran) giving the destination/placement. سوار is the object 'horsemen/mounted riders' (suitably postposed after the locative in poetic prose).
segments.s258-g166: ز is the poetic 'of/from' linking the noun to its source/constituents; گردان is 'troop/unit' and is modified by the following compound 'شمشیر زن' (literally 'sword-striker' = 'swordsman'). زن here is the deverbal/agent sense 'one who strikes' (not 'woman'). The numeral phrase 'سی هزار' (thirty thousand) quantifies the گردان (typical poetic ordering puts the numeral after the noun).
segments.s258-g167: بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him' ('be‑+o'), so بدو گفت = 'he said to him'. بردار is an imperative 'take up!' and is followed by the noun phrase شمشیر کین 'the sword of vengeance' (شمشیر modified by the genitive/postmodifier کین). Verb 'گفت' here is the past-reporting verb 'said' (placed between the indirect object and the quoted imperative).
segments.s258-g168: وز is a poetic/archaic preposition (here functioning like از/بر — 'against' / 'upon' / 'of') introducing the target group; ایشان is 'them'. بپرداز is an imperative 'deal with/attack/strike' (the b‑prefix marks the verbal stem in this form). روی زمین is a locative 'on the ground' (روی = 'on/upon', زمین = 'ground/earth'), giving the manner/place of the action.
segments.s258-g105: Imperative clause with the verb بشویی (imperative 2nd singular of the root 'شستن' here used figuratively) taking دل as its object and the prepositional phrase از مهر افراسیاب marking what the heart should be cleansed of. The word order is verb–object–prepositional phrase, typical in formal/poetic Persian imperatives.
segments.s258-g106: Negative present/subjunctive verb نبینی (2nd singular) introduces the assertion; the following nominal group شب تیره او را به خواب (night + adjective + object marker + locative phrase) is the thing not seen. را is the definite/object marker attached to او; به خواب here means 'in sleep / in a dream'. Poetic word order places the temporal/qualifying phrase (شب تیره) before the locative.
segments.s258-g107: Conditional protasis introduced by the particle گر ('if'); the phrase از شاه ترکان is a genitive/agent phrase ('of/from the king of the Turks') giving the object of the verb بترسی (2nd singular present/subjunctive of 'ترسیدن' = 'to fear'). ز بد is a short poetic causal phrase 'because of (his) evil'.
segments.s258-g108: نتیجه (apodosis) begins with the negative-future/volitional form نخواهی ('you will not want/wish'), followed by a that-clause (که + آیی) expressing the content 'that you come to Iran'. The clause ends with the impersonal predicate سزد ('it befits' / 'it is fitting'), which comments on the preceding idea; this is a common classical construction where an impersonal verb closes a subordinate clause.
segments.s258-g109: Two imperatives joined by و: بپرداز (imperative), here meaning 'set about / attend to / turn (your action) toward', takes the place-name توران as its object; بنشین is a second imperative 'sit down / settle' and is followed by the locative phrase به چاج ('at Chaj'), where چاج is a (probably proper) place-name in this context.
segments.s258-g110: Imperative sequence: ببر is imperative 'take!' (here the verb, not the animal), with تخت ساج as its direct object 'the Sāj throne' (ساج likely names the wood/type or a specific throne). The second clause بر افراز تاج uses the compound imperative sense of بر + افراز (raise/erect) meaning 'uplift/hold up the crown' — افراز functions as a verbal stem used imperatively and بر is the prepositional/particle signaling elevation.
segments.s258-g111: Nominal statement where ورت ('course/movement' or 'turn') is the subject and سوی افراسیابست رای is a verbless copular clause: افراسیابست is the fused name+'st' (is Afrasiab) and رای ('counsel/opinion') characterizes the subject — overall meaning 'your course/counsel is toward Afrasiab' (i.e. 'your counsel is to go to Afrasiab'). Note the poetic fusion of the copula to the name.
segments.s258-g112: Imperative followed by an admonition: برو سوی او is a plain imperative 'go toward him.' جنگ ما را is the object phrase 'our war' marked by را. The final word مپای is an archaic/rare form (here printed as a single orthographic item); in context it functions as a prohibitive/negational admonition attached to جنگ ما را ('do not [neglect/abandon/let go of] our war'). The exact lexical meaning of مپای is rare/uncertain, but grammatically the clause is an imperative admonishing care toward 'our war'.
segments.s258-g33: از آن کو = a classical relative phrase 'from that one who / from him who'. 'از' is the preposition 'from'; 'آن' is the demonstrative 'that'; 'کو' is a classical relative/ interrogative particle (here functioning like 'who') — the sense 'who' is not in the provided list so it is irregular/archaic. 'به کار سیاوش' is a prepositional phrase 'regarding the affair (کار) of Siavash' placed before the predicate; 'رد' is the noun 'rejection' and here functions as the object/idea being cast away. Word order is typical classical Persian where modifiers and objects can precede the verb that follows.
segments.s258-g34: بیفگند is a classical third-person singular verb (bi‑ + fکند) meaning 'he threw/cast' (past action). یک روز is an adverbial time phrase 'one day/once' modifying the verb. بنیاد بد is the noun 'foundation' (بنیاد) with the adjective 'bad' (بد) following it — the object of بیفگند. The verb governs the preceding noun phrase (object-before-verb word order is common in poetry).
segments.s258-g35: به نزد is a classical locative/epistemic phrase 'in the sight of / with respect to'. منش here means 'origin' (i.e. lineage/origin) and is the noun governed by 'به نزد'. دستگاهست is a fused predicate ('دستگاه' = 'court/royal household' + 'ست' = 'is'), so the clause says 'by origin he is of the court'. نیز at the end is the adverb 'also/too'.
segments.s258-g36: ز is the poetic form of 'از' meaning 'of/with regard to' and takes the following noun phrase as the thing of which innocence is being asserted. خون پدر is a genitive construction 'his father's blood' (blood = خون, پدر = father). بیگناهست is the predicate 'is innocent' (بیگناه + ست). نیز again is 'also', paralleling the previous clause. So the structure: 'He is, likewise, innocent of his father's blood.'
segments.s258-g37: گناهی is an indefinite noun 'a sin'. که introduces a relative clause 'that'. تا این زمان is an adverbial phrase 'up to this time / until now'. کرده‌ای is the perfect (compound) form 'you have done' — کرده (past participle) + ای (2nd-singular auxiliary), so the clause reads 'a sin that you have done until now.'
segments.s258-g38: ز is the poetic variant of 'از' ('from/against/of') marking the target of the sin. شاهان گیتی is a genitive phrase 'kings of the world' (شاهان = kings, گیتی = world). که introduces a relative clause 'whom' and آزرده‌ای is the perfect 2nd-person form 'you have harmed/distressed' (آزرده + ای). The whole segment: 'against the kings of the world whom you have harmed.'
segments.s258-g39: همی is a classical adverbial particle (here an intensifier, 'thus/indeed' or continuous 'still'). شاه is the subject 'the king'. بگذارد is a third-person singular verb in classical form with the prefix ب‑ (here meaning 'will allow/let pass' or 'forgive/overlook'). از تو 'from you' marks the person affected/concerned and همه 'all' is the object (i.e. 'all (your) [evil]'). The clause means 'the king will let all of it pass concerning you.'
segments.s258-g40: بدی is 'evil/badness' (here echoed from the previous segment). نیکی انگارد is a verbal phrase: نیکی 'goodness' as the object and انگارد (classical present/optative of انگاشتن) 'he will regard/consider'. از تو 'from you' marks the source, and همه 'all' quantifies. Together: 'He will regard all goodness as coming from you.' The poetic order puts emphasis by fronting nouns and splitting the idea across the two segments.
segments.s258-g73: دگر is an adverb here meaning 'moreover' or 'again' and opens the clause. هرچ (هر چه) is an indefinite relative meaning 'whatever' and is followed by a genitive-like phrase از گنج 'of/from treasure.' نزدیک تست functions as the predicate: نزدیک (near) qualifies the preceding noun phrase and تست is the archaic/poetic copula (here representing 'توست' = 'is (to) you' or 'near you') that links the phrase to the speaker's addressee; the printed lemma 'تست' in the wordlist is not the ordinary noun 'test' but this poetic copula (sense not given), so treat it as a 2nd-person copular ending.
segments.s258-g74: همه is the subject 'all/every'; دشمن جان is a construct/genitive 'enemies of life' (دشمن governed by جان), so the phrase means 'all the enemies of the life/soul.' تاریک is a predicate adjective 'dark/gloomy.' تست again is the archaic copula meaning roughly 'are/are to you' or 'belong / pertain (to you)'—the supplied senses do not match this poetic copular use, so mark it as unmatched.
segments.s258-g75: ز introduces an enumerated source/list meaning 'from.' اسپان (اسبان) 'horses' is modified by the adjective پرمایه 'well-bred / substantial' (adjective follows the noun in this poetic order), and گوهران 'gems' completes this part of the list; the conjunction و links the coordinated items.
segments.s258-g76: Again ز marks 'from,' followed by دیبا 'brocade' and the coordinating و 'and' with دینار 'dinar(s)/money.' وز here is a poetic fused form ( و + ز ) meaning 'and from,' linking to the next noun افسران; the wordlist's senses for وز do not match this connective usage so treat it as the conjunctive 'and from.' افسران in this spoils list means 'helmets' (a common classical sense) rather than 'officers.'
segments.s258-g77: ز begins another item in the cumulative list 'from.' ترگ here denotes some kind of cloth/rag (a small garment or fragment) and is joined by و and another ز before شمشیر 'sword.' The final item برگستوان in this triplet is a compound term (literally 'on the shoulder' in formation) used here as a piece of accoutrement (arm-guard/shoulder-piece); the listed senses did not supply this meaning, so the precise sense is taken from the poetic context rather than the provided wordlist.
segments.s258-g78: The pattern continues with ز introducing another source item; خفتان in this enumerative context names another class of textiles or clothing (the provided sense 'to sleep' is the unrelated verb stem, so the noun sense is contextually understood and not supplied in the list). After the coordinating و, از is the regular preposition 'from' and خنجر 'dagger' is specified as of هندوان 'the Indians' (i.e., Indian daggers), where هندوان marks origin or style.
segments.s258-g79: همه 'all' quantifies the following nouns. آلت لشکر is a genitive construction meaning 'instruments/implements of the army' (i.e., war-equipment). The coordinated سیم و زر are 'silver and gold' (metal/wealth), forming a catalogue of spoils or valuables.
segments.s258-g80: فرستی is an archaic/poetic past form meaning 'you sent' (second-person past), derived from فرستادن; به نزدیک ما 'to near us / to our vicinity' uses به + مکان phrase, and سربه‌سر here functions adverbially meaning 'one and all / entirely / each and every one'—it stresses that everything was sent to them. Word order places the verb before the destination and adverbial for poetic effect.
segments.s258-g57: کزو is a subordinating/relative adverb meaning 'for which / because of which' that introduces the clause; شاه is the subject 'the king'; ما را places 'us' as the definite/direct object with the postposition را; به کین خواستن is an infinitival verbal phrase 'to desire/seek vengeance' where به + کین marks the motive/aim and خواستن appears in the infinitive form (here reported as the action 'seeking vengeance').
segments.s258-g58: نباید is an impersonal modal 'it must not / one should not'; بسی here is an adverb modifying لشکر (classically 'many / a great'), and لشکر is the noun 'army/host'; آراستن is an infinitive 'to array / to equip' so the clause literally says 'one should not (باید نـِ) array many an army' (i.e. 'should not muster a large force').
segments.s258-g59: مگر functions as 'unless' introducing the negative-exceptional clause; پند من is 'my advice' with the possessor following the noun; سربه‌سر is an adverb 'entirely / completely' modifying the verb; بشنوی is 2nd person singular present/subjunctive of شنیدن used here in a conditional sense 'you listen/heed (may you hear)' or 'you shall hear'.
segments.s258-g60: به گفتار هشیار من is a prepositional phrase 'to/in the speech (words) of my wisdom' where به marks direction/toward and گفتار هشیار من = 'my prudent words'; بگروی (from گرویدن) is 2nd person singular present/subjunctive/polite imperative 'you should adopt/turn to' — here meaning 'follow' or 'accept' that counsel.
segments.s258-g61: نخستین کسی 'the first person' is the head noun phrase; کو is a classical relative particle (here 'who / that') introducing the relative clause; پی افگند is a compound where پی ('in pursuit / after') combines with the past tense افگند ('he cast/threw') to mean 'he set after / pursued / set in motion' — together meaning 'who first set in motion / pursued'; کین is the object 'vengeance' of that action.
segments.s258-g62: به خون ریختن is an infinitival/verbal-noun phrase 'to spill / shed blood' (به marks the action's aim); برنوشت here functions syntactically as a past tense verb 'wrote upon' (بر + نوشت) in classical phrasing meaning 'wrote on', and آستین is the object 'sleeve' — so the segment reads 'he wrote “to spill blood” on his sleeve' (a poetic way to express a vow/inscription of vengeance).
segments.s258-g63: به خون سیاووش is a prepositional phrase 'at/over Siavash's blood' (به + noun in construct); یازید is past tense 'yāzīd' (to gape, break forth, or burst out) used here with a transitive/causative sense of 'stretched forth / attacked / broke out'; دست is the noun 'hand' which, in the poetic word order, follows the verb as the subject or agent ('hands sprang forth / reached').
segments.s258-g64: جهانی is the subject 'the world / all the world'; به بیداد is an adverbial phrase 'by injustice' (به + بیداد); بر کرد is a classical compound where بر (a prefixed preposition/particle) accompanies کردن to mean 'did / wrought upon'; پست is a predicate adjective 'base / despicable', so the clause says 'the world, by injustice, performed a base deed.'
segments.s258-g97: تو (subject) + نیز (adverb) + آنگهی (temporal adverb ‘then/at that time’) precede the verb برگزینی. برگزینی here functions narratively as ‘you chose’ (2nd-person) in Ferdowsi's epic tense/formation. دو راه is the direct object (‘two paths/ways’) placed after the verb in this poetic syntax.
segments.s258-g98: یکی introduces the first of two alternatives (‘one (is) …’). راه‌جویی is a deverbal noun meaning ‘seeking the way’ or ‘going in search’ and functions as the predicate of the enumeration. به نزدیک شاه is a directional phrase: به + نزدیک + شاه = ‘toward/into the vicinity of the king’ (نزدیک here as adjective/adverb ‘near’).
segments.s258-g99: ابا here conveys refusal/abandonment (‘leave aside, shun’) and takes دودمان (lineage, family) as its object. نزد خسرو شوی = ‘be/come into the presence of Khosrow’ — نزد ‘in the presence of’ + proper name; شوی is 2nd-person singular of شدن/شدن used as ‘become/go/be’ (here meaning ‘be at / present with’).
segments.s258-g100: بدان is the fused construction ب + دان meaning ‘know that’ (imperative/optative sense ‘know’). سایه مهر او = ‘the shade/shelter of his favour’ (سایه = shelter/protection; مهر = favour/affection). بغنوی (ب + غنوی) expresses the idea ‘you will sleep/rest’ (from غنودن, here used figuratively as finding repose under his protection).
segments.s258-g101: کنم is 1st-person singular of کردن ‘I make’ (here ‘I give/make (a pact)’). با تو marks the party to whom the pledge is made (‘with you’ = ‘to you’). پیمان is the direct object (‘a pact’). که introduces the subordinate clause reporting what the pact states; خسرو is the subject of that clause and ترا (تو + را) is the object (‘Khosrow … you’).
segments.s258-g102: به introduces the goal/destination (‘to/into’). خورشید تابان is a descriptive phrase ‘the sun, shining’ used metaphorically to qualify the destination; تابان is an adjectival participle ‘shining/bright’. برآرد is 3rd-person singular of برآوردن ‘bring/raise’ (here future/promissory ‘will bring/raise’). سرا is the direct object ‘house/palace’ (the thing he will raise you to).
segments.s258-g103: ز (poetic form of از) = ‘of/from’. مهر دل او is a compound genitive ‘the love/affection of his heart’ (مهر qualifies دل, with او as the possessor). تو is the subject and آگه‌تری is the comparative predicate ‘you are more aware/better informed’ — Persian omits the copula, so ‘تو آگه‌تری’ = ‘you are more aware’.
segments.s258-g104: کزو = ‘from which / from whom’ (refers back to مهرِ دلِ او). هیچ ناید = ‘nothing comes’ (هیچ = nothing; ناید here means ‘does come’ in the sense ‘comes’ with negative force giving ‘nothing comes’). جز از بهتری = ‘except from the better’ — جز (‘except’) + از (‘from’) + بهتری (comparative ‘the better’ / ‘that which is better’); together: ‘nothing comes from it except what is better.’
segments.s258-g113: 'اگر' introduces a conditional ('if'); 'تو' is the emphatic/explicit second‑person pronoun. 'بخواهی' is the present/subjunctive form meaning 'you wish/want' and sets the condition. 'بسیچید' is a verbal adjective/past participle used as a verbal noun meaning 'mustered / to muster' and is followed by its complement 'جنگ' ('the battle') — literally 'if you wish (to) muster the battle'.
segments.s258-g114: The phrase 'مرا ... است' is an older word order where the dative/topic pronoun ('مرا' = 'to me / for me') precedes the predicate. Here the predicate is nominal: 'زور' (strength) followed by the adjectival/predicative form 'شیرست' (lion‑like, 'is a lion'). The copula 'است' is stylistically fused/omitted. 'و' links a second nominal predicate 'چنگ پلنگ' (the claw/paw of a leopard) — together: 'my strength is a lion and (is) a leopard's claw.'
segments.s258-g115: 'به ترکان' is a benefactive/recipient prepositional phrase ('to the Turks'). 'نمانم' is the negative present/subjunctive/optative first‑person form meaning 'I will not remain / I will not be left' (here: 'I will not leave / be left for'). 'من' is an emphatic pronoun. 'از تخت بهر' is a prepositional phrase where 'از تخت' = 'from the throne' and 'بهر' (archaic) indicates purpose/benefit ('for'/'to the use of') — together: 'I will not leave the throne to the Turks.'
segments.s258-g116: 'کمان من' is 'my bow' (noun + possessive). 'ابرست' is a predicative/adjectival form meaning 'is cloud / cloud‑like' (copula understood). 'و' joins the second clause. 'بارانش' = 'its rain' (باران + enclitic possessive -ش). 'زهر' is the predicate 'poison' (again copula implied). Grammatically these are nominal predicates linked by 'و': 'my bow is a cloud, and its shower/rain is poison.'
segments.s258-g117: 'بسیچیده' is a past participle/adjectival form ('mustered, well arranged') modifying 'جنگ' ('the battle/host'). The following verbs 'خیز' and 'اندرآی' are archaic imperatives: 'خیز' = 'rise!' and 'اندرآی' = 'come in / enter!'. The noun phrase sets the scene/condition ('with the army mustered'), then the two commands urge action.
segments.s258-g118: 'گرت' is the contracted conditional particle meaning 'if you have / if there is to you'; 'هست' is the copula/existential 'is/are'. 'با' introduces accompaniment ('with'). 'شیر' is 'lion' and 'درنده' is an adjectival qualifier 'predatory/ferocious'; 'پای' literally 'foot/paw' modifies the lion image. Together the clause means 'if you have (or are accompanied by) a ferocious‑pawed lion.'
segments.s258-g119: 'چو' introduces a temporal clause ('when'). 'صف' is the battle‑line/formation. 'برکشید' is a past tense verb 'was/was drawn up, raised' (here 'the line was drawn up'). 'از دو رویه سپاه' is a prepositional phrase 'from/between the two faces/sides of the army' (رویه = face/side/manner) describing how the formation was arranged: 'when the ranks were drawn up on both sides of the host.'
segments.s258-g120: 'گنهکار' is the noun/adjective 'the guilty' (those who have sinned). 'پیدا شد' is a compound meaning 'became visible / appeared / were revealed.' 'از بیگناه' literally 'from the innocent' functions as a partitive/prepositional phrase meaning 'among the innocent' — so 'the guilty were revealed among the innocent.'
segments.s258-g145: بیامد is simple past 'he came'. چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when'; نزدیک جیحون رسید = 'reached near the Jihun' with رسید (past) as the finite verb and the subject omitted (implied). The clause order is typical Classical Persian: temporal marker (چو) + location phrase + verb.
segments.s258-g146: به گرد لب آب is a prepositional phrase meaning 'around/at the brink of the water' (به + گرد + لب آب). لشکر کشید is a verb phrase 'he drew up/deployed an army' where کشید is past tense 3rd singular of کشیدن; the object (لشکر) precedes the verb here in a natural Persian order.
segments.s258-g147: به جیحون sets the place 'at the Jihun'. پر از نیزه is the adjectival phrase 'full of spears' (پر + از + نیزه) modifying دیوار. دیوار کرد is 'made (a) wall' (کرد as the light verb meaning 'made/constructed') with the descriptive phrase before the verb, a normal Classical order.
segments.s258-g148: چو here = 'when'. با + names (با گیو گودرز) indicates accompaniment/participants 'with Giv and Goudarz'. دیدار is a noun 'meeting' used with the light verb کرد to mean 'met (with)'; this noun + کرد construction is common in Persian for 'to have a meeting'.
segments.s258-g149: دو هفته شد is an impersonal/time expression 'two weeks passed' (شد as auxiliary/predicative). اندر سخنشان درنگ = 'a delay/lingering in their talk' where اندر = 'in/among', سخنشان = 'their speech/talk', and درنگ = 'pause/delay'. The whole segment says that two weeks passed while they lingered in discussion.
segments.s258-g150: بدان is a classical connective meaning 'for that reason / to that end' (ب‑ as a prefix plus دان in older usage). تا introduces a purpose clause 'so that / in order that' and is followed by the negative subjunctive نباشد ('may not be' / 'there should not be'). به بیداد جنگ literally 'to/by injustice (بیداد) war' = 'an unjust war'; به here marks the manner ('by/through'). The clause thus gives the purpose: they delayed so that an unjust war would not occur.
segments.s258-g151: ز هر گونه گفتند = 'they spoke of every kind (of thing)' where ز = 'from/of' used poetically, هر گونه = 'every kind', and گفتند is the past plural 'they spoke/said' (here 'they discussed'). و پیران شنید = 'and the elders listened' (پیران = elders, شنید = past 'heard/listened').
segments.s258-g152: گنهکاری آمد ز ترکان پدید is a compact poetic sequence: گنهکاری آمد 'a sinner/sinful one came' and ز ترکان پدید 'appeared/from the Turks' (پدید here = 'appeared, emerged'). The noun گنهکاری functions as the appearing person, and the two verbs آمد and پدید together emphasize his sudden arrival/from among the Turks.
segments.s258-g89: و دیگر که opens the clause: و = 'and', دیگر که = 'and besides / and that'. پور گزین ترا is a nominal phrase: پور 'son' + گزین 'chosen' (here functioning adjectivally) with the 2nd‑person suffix ت + object marker را written as ترا (your). There is no explicit verb here (a common poetic ellipsis); the whole phrase supplies the subject/possessive ('your chosen son').
segments.s258-g90: This continues the predication left implicit in the previous segment: نگهبان گاه = 'guardian of the gāh/place/seat' (نگهبان = guardian). و links a second predicate. نگین ترا is a possessive nominal: نگین 'gem / signet' + ترا 'your' (ت + را). The verb is still omitted; these nouns serve as predicates describing the 'chosen son'.
segments.s258-g91: برادرت (برادر + ت) = 'your brother(s)' with the possessive suffix. The following هر دو سران سپاه means 'both (هر دو) the chiefs (سران) of the army (سپاه)' — i.e. 'both your brothers, the army's leaders.' Word order places the possessor before the quantified noun phrase.
segments.s258-g92: که introduces a relative clause 'who / which'. هزمان = 'simultaneously / at the same time'. برآرند is 3rd‑person plural present/subjunctive form meaning 'they raise/bring up' (here future/prophetic sense). گردن به ماه is a prepositional phrase 'neck toward the moon' (گردن = neck, به = toward/to, ماه = moon), completing the verb's object/goal.
segments.s258-g93: چو = 'when'. هر سه = 'all three' (literally 'each three', used as 'the three of them'). بدین is the fused form (ب + دین) functioning as 'in/at this' or 'with this' and نامدار modifies انجمن to give 'this renowned assembly / company.' The phrase sets a temporal/conditional frame for the following command.
segments.s258-g94: گروگان = 'a hostage'. فرستی here is a 2nd‑person form (imperative/subjunctive) meaning 'send' (you send). به نزدیک من = 'to near me' i.e. 'to my presence / to me' (به = to, نزدیک = near, من = me). Together it yields the command 'send a hostage to me.'
segments.s258-g95: بدان is here ب + دان used idiomatically as 'by that / with that' (the prepositional sense 'by/with' plus a demonstrative element); تا شوم = 'so that I may become' (تا = so that/in order that, شوم = 1st‑person present/subjunctive 'I become'). ایمن از کار تو = 'safe from your activity/doing' (ایمن = safe, از = from, کار تو = your deed/action). The clause expresses purpose/result.
segments.s258-g96: برآرد is 3rd‑person singular 'he/it brings forth' or 'will bring forth'. درخت وفا = 'the tree of fidelity/loyalty' (درخت = tree, وفا = fidelity). بار تو = 'your fruit/burden/produce' with تو = your; here بار has the sense of what the tree bears. This clause states the favorable result: the tree of loyalty will yield your load/fruit.
segments.s258-g81: به بیداد is a prepositional phrase: به + بیداد = 'to/into injustice' (here marking the thing being exalted). کز introduces a relative clause ('which/that'); مردمان is the object 'the people'; بستدی is an archaic past verb form 'bound/fastened' (2nd/3rd-person past in classical style) modifying بیداد via کز: 'injustice which you fastened on the people'.
segments.s258-g82: فراز آوریدی is a verb phrase: فراز (on high/aloft) + آوریدی (you brought/raised) = 'you raised on high'. ز دست بدی is an ablative/agent phrase 'from/by the hand of evil' (ز = 'from/by', دست here = agency/power, بدی = 'evil'), so the whole clause means 'and you raised/brought forth evil by your hand.'
segments.s258-g83: بدان is the imperative/hortative 'know this' formed from ب- + دان (be- + know). باز خری is a verbal phrase 'buy back / redeem' (باز = back/again, خری = 'you buy' / 'you may buy'); مگر here functions as 'provided that / if' (classical usage). جان خویش = 'your life' (جان = life; خویش = one's own, with ش as the pronominal clitic).
segments.s258-g84: از این در کنی is a conditional/subjunctive clause: از = 'from', این = 'this (place)', در = 'door/way' (so 'out of here'), کنی = 2nd-person subjunctive/imperative 'you do / you get out' — 'if you get out of here'. زود درمان خویش places the remedy/result: زود = 'quickly/soon', درمان = 'remedy/cure', خویش + ش = 'your own' (your cure). Together: 'if you quickly leave here you will (or can) obtain your cure.'
segments.s258-g85: چه here introduces a causal/explanatory clause ('since/because' in classical usage). اندر = 'in/within'. خور شهریارست is a nominal phrase: خور (literally 'one who eats' i.e. one who shares the royal table — hence a member of the court) + شهریارست ('is of the king' / 'kingly') = 'is of the king's household/court'. از آن links this clause to the next ('for that reason / therefore').
segments.s258-g86: فرستم is the 1st-person present/future verb 'I send' (classical present form). به نزدیک = 'to the near/near to' meaning 'into the presence of'; شاه جهان = 'the King of the World' (شاه = king, جهان = world). The clause states the narrator's action: 'I send him to the king's presence.'
segments.s258-g87: ببخشیم is 1st-person plural 'we forgive / we will forgive' (hortative/indicative in narrative). دیگر همه = 'all the others' or 'the rest, everyone else'. بر سپاه = 'upon/among the army/host' (بر marking the relation to the host). So: 'We will pardon all the others among the army.'
segments.s258-g88: به جای = 'in place of / instead of'. مکافات = 'retribution / punishment'. کرده گناه = a noun phrase 'the sin done' (کرده = 'done', a participle; گناه = 'sin'). Together this adds the reason/condition: 'instead of punishment for the sin they have done.'
segments.s258-g201: و دیگر که — a short temporal sequence: و + دیگر works as 'and then' (و = a coordinating/sequence particle here). که is the subordinating conjunction 'that/when' introducing the temporal clause. پیغام شاه آمدست is the clause 'the king's message came' with پیغامِ شاه (message of the king) as subject and آمدست, an archaic/past perfective form of آمدن, as the verb; the possessor شاه follows the possessed noun (typical Persian word order).
segments.s258-g202: به فرمان جنگم سپاه آمدست — a separate main clause: به فرمان here means 'by/on the order (of)', فرمان is 'order/command', and جنگم is used here in the sense of 'war/ fighting' tied to فرمان (an order to go to war); سپاه is the subject/object 'the army/host' and آمدست is the same archaic past form 'came/arrived'. Word order places the phrase marking cause/authority (به فرمان جنگم) before the verb.
segments.s258-g203: چو پاسخ چنین یافت برگشت گیو — چو = 'when/as'; پاسـخ چنین = 'such an answer' (چنین follows/qualifies پاسخ); یافت is the past of یافـتن 'found/received' and is the main verb of the temporal clause. برگشت is a following verb 'returned' and گیو (the proper name) appears after the verb (Persian allows postverbal subject). The whole means 'When he found such an answer, Giv returned.'
segments.s258-g204: ابا لشکری نامبردار و نیو — ابا here is an archaic verb/adjective meaning 'to refuse / be reluctant' and modifies the following noun phrase; لشکری is the military/troops (adjectival/attributive form of سپاه), نامبردار 'renowned' follows the noun as an adjective, and و نیو links an additional descriptor (نیو uncertain/archaic: either ’new’ or a named thing). Grammatically this whole short phrase qualifies Giv's action: he returned, (ابا) with the famed troops (and نیو).
segments.s258-g205: سپهدار چون گیو برگشت از اوی — سپهدار (title: 'the spahbed/commander') is the topic; چون here functions temporally ('when/as') introducing the subordinate clause 'چون گیو برگشت' = 'when Giv returned'. از اوی literally 'from him/from that side' — اوی is an archaic third‑person pronoun. Word order places the subordinate temporal clause in the middle of the line while keeping the title up front.
segments.s258-g206: خروشان سوی جنگ بنهاد روی — خروشان ('roaring,tumultuous') modifies the action; سوی جنگ = 'toward the battle' (سوی = toward, جنگ = battle). بنهاد is the past form of نهادن 'to place/put' and in the idiom روی بنهاد = 'to set (one's) face toward' = 'to head for', so the clause means 'he set his face roaring toward the battle.' The adjective precedes the directional phrase and the verbal idiom completes the motion.
segments.s258-g207: دمان از پس گیو پیران دلیر — دمان (literally 'skirt/hem', here figuratively 'the rear / those following') + از پس = 'from after/behind' gives 'following after Giv'. پیران دلیر = 'the brave elders' where پیران (elders) is qualified by the adjective دلیر 'brave' that follows it, a common adjective placement in classical Persian.
segments.s258-g208: سپه را همی راند بر سان شیر — سپه را: سپه 'army/host' with را as the definite/object marker. همی راند is the classical progressive/narrative form (همی + past stem) meaning 'was driving/led' or 'drove' (continuous past sense). بر سان شیر = 'upon/like a lion' (سان = 'like/as', شیر = 'lion') — together 'drove the host like a lion.'
segments.s258-g177: چو is a subordinating conjunction here meaning 'when' introducing a temporal clause. پیران is the subject ('the elders'); بدید is the simple past 3rd‑singular 'saw'. The demonstrative آن + noun سپاه بزرگ ('that/the great army') is the direct object; the usual object marker را is omitted (a frequent poetic omission), so word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s258-g178: به خون تشنه is an adjectival/prepositional phrase meaning 'thirsty for blood' (به = 'for/to', خون = 'blood', تشنه = 'thirsty'). هر یک = 'each one'. به کردار گرگ = 'in the manner/behavior of a wolf' (کردار = 'conduct/behavior'); here به before کردار is adverbial ('in the ... way'). Again the object marker is omitted and the phrase is compacted in poetic style.
segments.s258-g179: بر آشفت is a compound verbal expression: بر acts like a preverb/prefix and آشفت (past) = 'was stirred/was agitated' or 'flared up'. از آن پس که = 'after that, when' (از آن پس = 'after that', که introduces the following clause). نیرو گرفت is a past tense clause 'he gained/received strength' (گرفت = 'took/seized/received'). The subject is omitted (understood from context).
segments.s258-g180: هنرها is the plural subject 'the arts/virtues/skills'. بشست is the simple past 'washed' (3rd‑sg). The following phrase از دل آهو گرفت is coordinated: از = 'from/out of', دل آهو = 'the gazelle's heart', گرفت = 'he took/seized'. Poetic ellipsis omits conjunctions; taken together it means he cast off/washed away his erstwhile virtues and seized from the gazelle's heart.
segments.s258-g181: جفا پیشه گشت is an idiomatic causative/turning construction: X پیشه گشت = 'became a practitioner of X' or 'took X up as a practice', so جفا پیشه گشت = 'became cruel' or 'took up cruelty as a practice'. آن دل نیکخوی is the subject phrase 'that kind/noble‑natured heart' placed after the predicate for poetic effect (inversion). گشت is the past of شدن 'became'.
segments.s258-g182: پر اندیشه شد = 'became full of thought' where پر is a predicative adjective 'full (of)', placed before the noun اندیشه ('thought'); شد is 'became'. رزم کرد آرزوی is an inverted clause: literally 'battle made (its) desire' — کرد here functions as 'made/caused' or a light verb with رزم (battle) to mean 'made battle his desire' (آرزوی = 'wish/desire' follows the verb as its object due to poetic word order).
segments.s258-g183: به گیو marks the indirect object 'to Giv' (به + name). آنگهی ('then/thereupon') is an adverb; گفت is the past 'he said', introducing reported speech which follows. برخیز و رو are imperatives within that reported speech: برخیز = 'stand up / arise', و = 'and', رو = imperative 'turn/face' (here used as a verb 'turn / go').
segments.s258-g184: سوی is the preposition 'toward(s)'; پهلوان = 'the champion/hero'. سپه = 'the army/host' follows, and باز شو is an imperative 'become again / return' (باز 'again' + شو 'become' used here as 'go back / return'). Together the imperatives form the command 'turn toward the champion; return to the army.'
segments.s258-g25: پر از + N is a fixed adjectival phrase meaning "full of N." Here two parallel adjectives are stacked for emphasis: "پر از درد ایران" = "full of Iran's pain" and "پر از داغ شاه" = "full of the king's grief." The phrases are fronted (no explicit noun they modify appears in this segment) and function as descriptive grounds that the following relative clause will comment on.
segments.s258-g26: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause ("which/that"). با سوک ایرج is an instrumental/causal prepositional phrase ("with/by the mourning of Iraj"); سوک is an archaic/poetic word for "mourning." نتابید is the simple past-negative of تابیدن in the sense "endure/bear," and ماه ("the moon" or a beautiful one) is placed after the verb for poetic inversion — literal order would be ماه نتابید, "the moon did not endure."
segments.s258-g27: ز here is the poetic variant of از meaning "from/of/among." The phrase orders the group first (ز ترکان) then the person (تو تنها) and finally the subset (از آن انجمن) — literally "of the Turks, you alone, from that assembly." The word تنها emphasizes exclusivity: "you alone (of that group)."
segments.s258-g28: شناسی is a present-tense form derived from شناخت/شناسیدن; in this context it reads as a predicative verb meaning "(are) known/recognised" or "you know" depending on interpretation — Persian often allows this ambiguity. به introduces the manner/quality ("by/with love and fidelity"), and خویشتن refers back to "your own/self." The whole group means "(you) are known (in that company) by/for your love and fidelity toward your own."
segments.s258-g29: دروغست is a copular clause meaning "it is false / it is a lie." بر تو is a prepositional phrase indicating the target/subject of the falsehood ("about you / as to you"). همین نام مهر literally "this very name 'Mehr' (i.e. 'love')" is the predicate being judged false about you; the predicate-before-subject order here is emphatic/poetic.
segments.s258-g30: نبینم is the negative present-subjunctive/optative form meaning "I do not see" or "may I not see," used here as a straightforward "I see not." به دلت (به + دل + ت) is literally "to your heart" (دلت = دل + second-person suffix), and اندر is an archaic locative preposition meaning "in/within." آرام مهر is a noun phrase "calm/peaceful love/affection." The clause thus says the speaker finds no peaceful love in the addressee's heart.
segments.s258-g31: همانست is the emphatic copula "it is the same." کان is an archaic/linking particle (here functioning like "when/that" or "as when") introducing the clause about the king. شاه آزرمجوی is a noun plus adjectival compound: "the king, (a) seeker of honour/repute," where آزرمجوی is a poetic adjective formed with -جو meaning "seeker of."
segments.s258-g32: مرا گفت is a common word order where the object (مرا, "me") comes before گفت ("said"); together they mean "he said to me." با او "with him / in dealings with him" + همه "in all (things)" set the scope, and نرم گوی is an imperative phrase: نرم (adverb/adjective "gently") + گوی (archaic imperative/infinitive of گفتن "say/speak") meaning "speak/greet gently" or here "be gentle." The sentence quotes a command: "Be gentle with him in everything."
